content:
How This Applies to Whiplash

The premise of Whiplash is deceptively simple:
• Andrew Neiman, a 19-year-old jazz drummer, is pushed to his limit by his ruthless instructor, Terence Fletcher.
• The film isn’t about music—it’s about obsession, power, and self-destruction.
• What makes it gripping is its execution:
• The tension is structured like a thriller—each scene escalates conflict.
• The protagonist is forced into high-stakes decisions that change him.

📌 McKee’s Principle Applied:
• Any story—no matter how simple—can be electrifying if structured well.
• Conflict, pacing, and character choices drive engagement, not just theme or subject matter.

2. Conflict Drives Story

McKee states that without conflict, there is no movement in a story. Every scene should present:
1. Internal Conflict → The protagonist fights against their own doubts, trauma, or limits.
